legacy-mode runs; the rejects-format feature only amends it for runs that have opted
into a new rejects-format shape. Downstream consumers that never pass
`--rejects-format` observe no the rejects-format feature changes to this sidecar at all.

Because the summary-schema-digest (`--summary-schema-digest`) and
summary-schema-index (`--summary-schema-index`) both derive from the
summary-schema-out bytes, both sidecars reflect the conditional
emission deterministically: legacy-mode digests/indices are
byte-identical to the summary-schema feature; tagged/tagged-sorted digests/indices reflect
the 7-key shape.

## 12. Breaking change — tagged `raw_line` for blank-only input lines

default base behavior defined the NDJSON reader as "collect non-blank lines". Under
the rejects-format feature's tagged / tagged-sorted projections, blank-only input lines
(lines that are empty after whitespace-trim, but are still physically
present in the shard) are RESCUED into the invalid bucket with
`reason_code: schema_version` and `raw_line` emitted as the literal
3-character string `"-"` (a single ASCII hyphen, wrapped in no quotes
and no escaping).

Under `--rejects-format legacy`: behavior is unchanged — blank-only
lines are SKIPPED, never reach a bucket, and never appear in
`rejects.txt`. Existing inputs remain byte-identical.

Under `--rejects-format tagged` and `--rejects-format tagged-sorted`:
blank-only lines are EMITTED as tagged reject rows with
`bucket=invalid`, `reason_code=schema_version`, `shard=<basename>`,
`line_no=<1-based>`, `raw_line="-"`. The determinism contract (§4)
still holds because the 1-based line index is stable within the
shard.

## 13. Summary of the rejects-format feature exits

| Condition | Exit | Stderr (exact) |
|---|---|---|
| `--rejects-format <unknown>` | 2 | `ingest: --rejects-format: must be one of {legacy, tagged, tagged-sorted}` |
| `--rejects-format tagged` without `--audit-log` | 2 | `ingest: --rejects-format tagged requires --audit-log` |
| `--rejects-format tagged-sorted` without `--audit-log` | 2 | `ingest: --rejects-format tagged-sorted requires --audit-log` |
| `--rejects-format-header` without `--rejects-format tagged` or `--rejects-format tagged-sorted` | 2 | `ingest: --rejects-format-header requires --rejects-format tagged` |

rounds.title: Configurable Session Policy
rounds.md:
# Configurable Session Policy

Add `--session-policy <gap|fixed-window|hybrid>` and
`--fixed-window-seconds <int>` to control how `summary.sessions.total` is
computed.

The default `gap` policy preserves the existing inactivity-based rule: start a
new session whenever the gap between consecutive surviving events for a user is
strictly greater than `--session-gap`, with open-session carry-over across
runs. `fixed-window` replaces that rule for `summary.sessions.total` by
bucketting each user's surviving events into fixed-duration windows anchored at
the user's first surviving event's floored timestamp. `hybrid` combines both
rules: either an excessive inactivity gap or a fixed-window boundary starts a
new session.

The policy affects only `summary.sessions.total`. It does not change
`state.open_sessions[].session_index` or audit-log `session_index`; those fields
continue to use the existing gap-based numbering. Every other output remains
byte-for-byte identical for a fixed input and flag set, aside from downstream
manifest/signature propagation when the summary session count changes.

## 1. Flag syntax

```
eventq ingest ... [--session-policy <gap|fixed-window|hybrid>] \
              [--fixed-window-seconds <int>]
```

- `--session-policy` is **optional**. Omitting it is exactly equivalent
  to supplying `--session-policy gap`. The default is `gap`.
- The flag value MUST be one of the three string literals `gap`,
  `fixed-window`, or `hybrid`. Any other value (including the empty
  string, prefixes like `fixed`, case variants like `Gap`, or any other
  token) is a usage error: exit code `2`, no output files created
  (neither `--output` nor `--rejects` nor any sidecar), and this exact
  stderr line:

  ```
  ingest: --session-policy: must be one of {gap, fixed-window, hybrid}
  ```

- `--session-policy` with no argument value (e.g. `--session-policy` as
  the final token) is also a usage error and must exit `2`; Go's flag
  parser emits its own usage error in that case. The exact stderr line
  for that empty/missing-value case is not part of the portable contract.
- `--fixed-window-seconds` is **optional** in general but **required**
  whenever `--session-policy` is `fixed-window` or `hybrid`:
  - Supplying `--session-policy fixed-window` without
    `--fixed-window-seconds` → exit code `2`, no output files created,
    and this exact stderr line:

    ```
    ingest: --session-policy fixed-window requires --fixed-window-seconds
    ```

  - Supplying `--session-policy hybrid` without `--fixed-window-seconds`
    → exit code `2`, no output files created, and this exact stderr
    line:

    ```
    ingest: --session-policy hybrid requires --fixed-window-seconds
    ```

- `--fixed-window-seconds` value must be a **positive** integer (`>= 1`).
  Zero or negative values supplied together with `--session-policy
  fixed-window` or `--session-policy hybrid` are a usage error: exit
  code `2`, no output files created, and this exact stderr line:

  ```
  ingest: --fixed-window-seconds: must be a positive integer
  ```

  (Go's flag parser naturally rejects non-integer tokens with its own
  usage error before this check fires; the fixed stderr above is for
  the case where the value parses as `0` or a negative integer.)
- Supplying `--fixed-window-seconds` together with `--session-policy gap`
  (or when `--session-policy` is omitted entirely) is a usage error:
  exit code `2`, no output files created, and this exact stderr line:

  ```
  ingest: --fixed-window-seconds requires --session-policy fixed-window or hybrid
  ```

- Validation order: the enum-value check on `--session-policy` runs at
  argument-parse time; the dependency checks between `--session-policy`
  and `--fixed-window-seconds` both run **before** any output file is
  written (including any `--output.tmp` or `--rejects.tmp`).
- `--session-policy` and `--fixed-window-seconds` compose freely with
  every existing flag.

## 2. `gap` policy (default; byte-identical with existing session behavior)

Under `--session-policy gap` (or when the flag is omitted), the
sessionization rule is the existing inactivity-based rule with cross-run
carry-over layered on top. For existing inputs and flag combinations, the
following outputs are byte-for-byte identical to the default behavior:

- `--output` (including `summary.sessions.total` and
  `summary.sessions.by_user[]`).
- `--rejects` (under any `--rejects-format`).
- `--state-out` (including `state.open_sessions[].session_index`).
- `--audit-log` (including per-record `session_index`).
- `--audit-log-digest`.
- `--out-parquet-manifest`, `--manifest-signature`.
- `--summary-schema-out`, `--summary-schema-digest`,
  `--summary-schema-index`.

The session-policy flag does NOT change a single byte of any existing output under
default `gap` policy on any existing input.

## 3. `fixed-window` policy (CONFLICT path)

Under `--session-policy fixed-window`, the original sole-gap session rule
is **replaced** — for the sole purpose of computing
`summary.sessions.total` — by a fixed-window bucketing rule.

Let `W = --fixed-window-seconds` (positive integer). For each user `u`
present in the current run's surviving events, consider `u`'s surviving
events sorted by `(ts ascending, arrival order for ties)` — the same
ordering used by the existing gap-session contract. Let `anchor_u` be the epoch-second
value of `u`'s first surviving event's `ts` floored to a multiple of
`W`: `anchor_u = floor(first_ts_epoch / W) * W`. Each subsequent event
is assigned to window `window_k(u, W) = anchor_u + k*W` where `k` is
the largest non-negative integer such that the event's `ts` epoch is
`>= anchor_u + k*W`. A new session begins whenever two consecutive
events for `u` fall into different windows (i.e. their `k` indices
differ). The first event for `u` always starts session 1.

`summary.sessions.total` under `fixed-window` is the sum across all
users of the number of distinct windows spanned by `u`'s surviving
events.

### 3.1 Fields that change under `fixed-window`

- `summary.sessions.total` — recomputed as above. This is the sole
  field in `--output` whose value differs from the default policy for
  any input that contains at least one user whose surviving events
  span more than one window.

### 3.2 Fields that do NOT change under `fixed-window`

- `summary.sessions.by_user[]` entries are byte-identical to the
  default-policy output (the per-user aggregate is over events, not
  sessions).
- `state.open_sessions[].session_index` retains the existing
  gap-based numbering. The fixed-window rule does NOT renumber
  open-session carry-over.
- Audit-log `session_index` (the audit-log feature §Record shape) retains the
  existing gap-based numbering. The fixed-window rule does NOT renumber
  audit records.
- Every sidecar (`--audit-log-digest`, `--summary-schema-out`,
  `--summary-schema-digest`, `--summary-schema-index`) is
  byte-identical to the default-policy output.
- `--out-parquet-manifest`'s `outputs.summary.sha256` and `.bytes`
  naturally reflect the new `--output` bytes when `summary.sessions.total`
  changed; `--manifest-signature` reflects the new manifest bytes. When
  `summary.sessions.total` happens to be the same under both policies
  (e.g. inputs where no user's events span a window boundary), the
  manifest and signature are byte-identical to the default-policy
  output as well.

### 3.3 Worked example

Fixture: one user `u1` with three surviving events in the same run:

```
t=100  type=purchase  amount=1 USD
t=160  type=login
t=300  type=purchase  amount=2 USD
```

- Under `--session-gap 120` (default `gap` policy):
  - gap(100→160) = 60, not greater than 120 → same session.
  - gap(160→300) = 140, greater than 120 → new session.
  - `summary.sessions.total = 2`.
- Under `--session-policy fixed-window --fixed-window-seconds 180`:
  - `anchor_u1 = floor(100 / 180) * 180 = 0`.
  - t=100 → k=0 (window [0, 180)), session 1.
  - t=160 → k=0 (window [0, 180)), same session.
  - t=300 → k=1 (window [180, 360)), new session.
  - `summary.sessions.total = 2`.
- Under `--session-policy fixed-window --fixed-window-seconds 120`:
  - `anchor_u1 = floor(100 / 120) * 120 = 0`.
  - t=100 → k=0 (window [0, 120)), session 1.
  - t=160 → k=1 (window [120, 240)), new session.
  - t=300 → k=2 (window [240, 360)), new session.
  - `summary.sessions.total = 3`.
- Under `--session-policy hybrid --session-gap 120 --fixed-window-seconds 180`:
  - gap rule splits at 160→300 (new session).
  - fixed-window rule splits at 160→300 (k=0→k=1, new session).
  - union of boundaries → 2 sessions.
  - `summary.sessions.total = 2`.
- Under `--session-policy hybrid --session-gap 120 --fixed-window-seconds 120`:
  - gap rule splits at 160→300.
  - fixed-window rule splits at 100→160 (k=0→k=1) AND 160→300 (k=1→k=2).
  - union of boundaries → 3 sessions.
  - `summary.sessions.total = 3`.

## 4. `hybrid` policy (CONFLICT path)

Under `--session-policy hybrid --fixed-window-seconds W`, a new session
begins whenever EITHER of the following conditions holds for two
consecutive surviving events `e_prev` and `e_cur` of a single user:

- `e_cur.ts_epoch - e_prev.ts_epoch > --session-gap` (default base behavior gap rule), OR
- the fixed-window index of `e_cur` differs from the fixed-window index
  of `e_prev` (the window rule, with `anchor_u` and `W` as in §3).

Equivalently, the per-user session count under `hybrid` is one more
than the number of indices `i` in the sorted event sequence where
either boundary condition fires.

Other than `summary.sessions.total`, `hybrid` preserves the same field
invariants as `fixed-window`: `by_user[]`, state, audit-log, and all
sidecars are byte-identical to the default-policy output, modulo the
manifest/signature propagation of a changed `summary.sessions.total`
integer.

## 5. Determinism

Given identical `--input-dir`, `--rates`, `--session-gap`,
`--watermark-lag`, any `--state-in`, any existing flag combination,
any `--session-policy` value, and any `--fixed-window-seconds` value,
`--output` is byte-identical across repeated runs. The fixed-window
bucketing is a pure arithmetic function of the user's first surviving
event's `ts` and the subsequent events' `ts` values, all of which are
deterministic under default base behavior.

## 6. Back-compat invariants (strict)

For every existing pre-policy input:

- Omitting `--session-policy` OR passing `--session-policy gap` →
  byte-identical `--output`, `--rejects`, `--state-out`, `--audit-log`,
  `--audit-log-digest`, `--out-parquet-manifest`, `--manifest-signature`,
  `--summary-schema-out`, `--summary-schema-digest`,
  `--summary-schema-index` vs. the rejects-format feature CLI.
- Passing `--session-policy fixed-window --fixed-window-seconds W` (for
  any valid `W`) → byte-identical `--rejects`, `--state-out`,
  `--audit-log`, `--audit-log-digest`, `--summary-schema-out`,
  `--summary-schema-digest`, `--summary-schema-index` vs. the
  default-policy invocation. `--output` may differ (only the
  `summary.sessions.total` integer changes when the input contains a
  user whose events span a window boundary). `--out-parquet-manifest`
  and `--manifest-signature` track any change in `--output` bytes.
- Passing `--session-policy hybrid --fixed-window-seconds W` → same as
  `fixed-window` with respect to back-compat invariants.

The session-policy flags do NOT add any new key to any `--output`, `--rejects`,
`--state-out`, `--audit-log`, `--audit-log-digest`,
`--out-parquet-manifest`, `--manifest-signature`, or any of the
summary-schema sidecars under any policy value. The only field whose
VALUE can change between policies is the integer
`summary.sessions.total` (and the manifest/signature bytes that track
`--output`).

## 7. Multi-field invariants (always, under every policy)

- `summary.sessions.total` is a non-negative integer for every policy.
- `summary.sessions.total >= (number of users with at least one
  surviving event)` under every policy (each surviving user contributes
  at least one session).
- `summary.sessions.by_user[].count` equals the number of surviving
  events for that user, under every policy (unchanged from default base behavior).
- Under `hybrid`: for any input and any `W`,
  `summary.sessions.total >= max(gap_policy_total, fixed_window_policy_total)`.

## 8. Usage-error rules (summary)

| Condition | Exit | Stderr (exact) |
|---|---|---|
| `--session-policy <unknown>` | 2 | `ingest: --session-policy: must be one of {gap, fixed-window, hybrid}` |
| `--session-policy fixed-window` without `--fixed-window-seconds` | 2 | `ingest: --session-policy fixed-window requires --fixed-window-seconds` |
| `--session-policy hybrid` without `--fixed-window-seconds` | 2 | `ingest: --session-policy hybrid requires --fixed-window-seconds` |
| `--fixed-window-seconds` supplied with `--session-policy gap` or omitted | 2 | `ingest: --fixed-window-seconds requires --session-policy fixed-window or hybrid` |
| `--fixed-window-seconds 0` or negative with `fixed-window` or `hybrid` | 2 | `ingest: --fixed-window-seconds: must be a positive integer` |

Every other existing usage-error rule is preserved.

## 9. Usage

```
eventq ingest --input-dir <dir> --rates <path> --output <path> --rejects <path> \
              --session-gap <seconds> --watermark-lag <seconds> \
              [--state-in <path>] [--state-out <path>] [--pending-ttl <seconds>] \
              [--max-open-sessions <int>] [--state-strict] \
              [--audit-log <path>] [--audit-log-digest <path>] \
              [--audit-log-rotate-bytes <int>] \
              [--rates-strict] [--rates-max-staleness <seconds>] \
              [--ingest-id <string>] [--out-parquet-manifest <path>] \
              [--manifest-signature <path>] \
              [--dedup-policy <first-arrival|lex-largest-ts>] \
              [--strict-late-audit] \
              [--summary-schema-out <path>] \
              [--summary-schema-digest <path>] \
              [--summary-schema-strict] \
              [--summary-schema-index <path>] \
              [--rejects-format <legacy|tagged|tagged-sorted>] \
              [--rejects-format-header] \
              [--session-policy <gap|fixed-window|hybrid>] \
              [--fixed-window-seconds <int>]
```

rounds.title: Sharding Report Sidecars
rounds.md:
# Sharding Report Sidecars

The manifest's `input_shards` array gives only a coarse per-shard summary.
Add a standalone sharding-report family for consumers that need per-shard
bytes, hashes, bucket counts, and observed timestamp ranges without
re-parsing `--audit-log` or cross-referencing `--rejects`.

`eventq ingest` now accepts exactly six new optional flags:
`--sharding-report`, `--sharding-report-digest`,
`--sharding-report-index`, `--sharding-report-histogram`,
`--sharding-report-sort`, and `--sharding-report-strict`. When all six flags
are absent, existing outputs are byte-for-byte unchanged. When any of these
flags are supplied, existing outputs remain identical to a run without them;
only the requested sidecars appear.

The report can be sorted by basename, byte size, or sha256. The default
`basename` sort preserves the existing basename-ascending behavior. The
`bytes` and `sha256` modes reorder the `shards[]` array, and dependent sidecar
fields such as JSONPath indices and report digests naturally reflect that
chosen order.

## 1. Flag syntax

```
eventq ingest ... [--sharding-report <path>]
                   [--sharding-report-digest <path>]
                   [--sharding-report-index <path>]
                   [--sharding-report-histogram <path>]
                   [--sharding-report-sort <basename|bytes|sha256>]
                   [--sharding-report-strict]
```

### 1.1 `--sharding-report <path>`

- `--sharding-report` is **optional** and takes a **single string value**
  (a filesystem path). It is absent by default.
- When `--sharding-report` is absent, the CLI does NOT create the
  file at any path; no `--sharding-report` byte stream exists on disk,
  and every existing output remains byte-identical to the default run.
- When `--sharding-report` is supplied, the value MUST be a non-empty
  string. An empty value (`--sharding-report=` or `--sharding-report ""`)
  is a usage error: exit code `2`, no output files created (neither
  `--output` nor `--rejects` nor any sidecar), and this exact stderr
  line:

  ```
  ingest: --sharding-report: value must be non-empty
  ```

- The parent directory of the supplied `--sharding-report` path MUST
  already exist. If it does not exist, exit `2`, no output files created,
  stderr (exact):

  ```
  ingest --sharding-report parent directory does not exist
  ```

- `--sharding-report` composes freely with every existing flag. In
  particular it is NOT gated on `--out-parquet-manifest`,
  `--audit-log`, `--summary-schema-out`, `--session-policy`, or
  `--rejects-format`. A run that passes only
  `--input-dir/--rates/--output/--rejects/--session-gap/--watermark-lag/--sharding-report`
  is valid.
- Validation order: the empty-value check runs at argument-parse time;
  the parent-directory-exists check runs **before** any output file is
  written (including any `--output.tmp` or `--rejects.tmp`).

### 1.2 `--sharding-report-digest <path>`

- `--sharding-report-digest` is **optional** and takes a **single string
  value** (a filesystem path). It is absent by default.
- When absent, the CLI does NOT create the digest file at any path.
- When supplied, the value MUST be a non-empty string. An empty value
  is a usage error: exit code `2`, no output files created, exact
  stderr line:

  ```
  ingest: --sharding-report-digest: value must be non-empty
  ```

- `--sharding-report-digest` REQUIRES `--sharding-report`. Supplying
  `--sharding-report-digest` without `--sharding-report` is a usage
  error: exit code `2`, no output files created, exact stderr line:

  ```
  ingest: --sharding-report-digest requires --sharding-report
  ```

- The parent directory of the supplied path MUST already exist. If it
  does not, exit `2`, no output files created, exact stderr line:

  ```
  ingest --sharding-report-digest parent directory does not exist
  ```

- Validation order: empty-value check → requires-sharding-report check
  → parent-directory-exists check. All three run BEFORE any output
  file is written.

### 1.3 `--sharding-report-index <path>`

- `--sharding-report-index` is **optional** and takes a **single string
  value** (a filesystem path). It is absent by default.
- When absent, the CLI does NOT create the index file at any path.
- When supplied, the value MUST be a non-empty string. An empty value
  is a usage error: exit code `2`, no output files created, exact
  stderr line:

  ```
  ingest: --sharding-report-index: value must be non-empty
  ```

- `--sharding-report-index` REQUIRES BOTH `--sharding-report` AND
  `--sharding-report-digest`. Supplying `--sharding-report-index`
  without either is a usage error: exit code `2`, no output files
  created, exact stderr line:

  ```
  ingest: --sharding-report-index requires --sharding-report and --sharding-report-digest
  ```

- The parent directory of the supplied path MUST already exist. If it
  does not, exit `2`, no output files created, exact stderr line:

  ```
  ingest --sharding-report-index parent directory does not exist
  ```

- Validation order: empty-value check → requires-both check →
  parent-directory-exists check.

### 1.4 `--sharding-report-histogram <path>`

- `--sharding-report-histogram` is **optional** and takes a **single
  string value** (a filesystem path). It is absent by default.
- When absent, the CLI does NOT create the histogram file at any
  path.
- When supplied, the value MUST be a non-empty string. An empty value
  is a usage error: exit code `2`, no output files created, exact
  stderr line:

  ```
  ingest: --sharding-report-histogram: value must be non-empty
  ```

- `--sharding-report-histogram` REQUIRES `--sharding-report` (it does
  NOT require `--sharding-report-digest` or `--sharding-report-index`,
  and composes independently with them). Supplying
  `--sharding-report-histogram` without `--sharding-report` is a
  usage error: exit code `2`, no output files created, exact stderr
  line:

  ```
  ingest: --sharding-report-histogram requires --sharding-report
  ```

- The parent directory of the supplied path MUST already exist. If it
  does not, exit `2`, no output files created, exact stderr line:

  ```
  ingest --sharding-report-histogram parent directory does not exist
  ```

- Validation order: empty-value check → requires-sharding-report
  check → parent-directory-exists check. All three run BEFORE any
  output file is written.

### 1.5 `--sharding-report-sort <basename|bytes|sha256>`

- `--sharding-report-sort` is **optional** and takes a **single string
  value**. Default `basename` (applied when the flag is absent or
  explicitly set to `basename`).
- When supplied, the value MUST be a non-empty string. An empty value
  (`--sharding-report-sort=` or `--sharding-report-sort ""`) is a
  usage error: exit code `2`, no output files created, exact stderr
  line:

  ```
  ingest: --sharding-report-sort: value must be non-empty
  ```

- `--sharding-report-sort` REQUIRES `--sharding-report`. Supplying it
  without `--sharding-report` is a usage error: exit `2`, exact stderr:

  ```
  ingest: --sharding-report-sort requires --sharding-report
  ```

- The value MUST be one of `basename`, `bytes`, or `sha256`. Any other
  value (e.g. `ts`, `size`, uppercase `BYTES`) is a usage error: exit
  `2`, no output files created, exact stderr:

  ```
  ingest: --sharding-report-sort: value must be one of basename|bytes|sha256
  ```

- Under `basename` (default), the sharding-report's `shards[]` array
  is sorted by the shard's basename in ASCII-ascending order — exactly
  the default basename-sorted behavior. When the flag is absent OR when its value is
  explicitly `basename`, every sidecar (`--sharding-report`,
  `--sharding-report-digest`, `--sharding-report-index`,
  `--sharding-report-histogram`) is byte-identical to the default
  basename-sorted state.
- Under `bytes`, `shards[]` is sorted ascending by the shard's `bytes`
  field, with `shard` (basename) as a deterministic tiebreaker when
  two shards have equal `bytes`.
- Under `sha256`, `shards[]` is sorted ascending by the lowercase-hex
  `sha256` string, with `shard` (basename) as tiebreaker.
- When `bytes` or `sha256` is chosen, the sharding-report's `shards[]`
  array is reordered. As downstream consequences:
  - `--sharding-report-index` emits `$.shards[<i>]` paths that reflect
    the new order (the 0-based position `<i>` is the shard's position
    in the reordered `shards[]` array).
  - `--sharding-report-digest`'s `report_sha256` and `report_bytes`
    reflect the new report bytes (because the report bytes change
    when `shards[]` is reordered).
  - `--sharding-report-histogram` values are computed over the per-shard
    vector in the new order; however, the histogram's buckets (counts,
    upper_bound sequence) do NOT depend on the enumeration order (the
    bucket is a count of how many shards fall into each range), so
    the histogram bytes are byte-identical across sort choices given
    identical shards contents.
- The `totals` object is computed over the same multiset of shards
  and is byte-identical across sort choices.
- Validation order: empty-value check → requires-sharding-report
  check → enum-value check.

### 1.6 `--sharding-report-strict`

- `--sharding-report-strict` is **optional** and is a **boolean flag**
  (no value; pass `--sharding-report-strict` to enable).
- When absent, strict mode is off; every sidecar is byte-identical to
  the default report state given the same sort key.
- `--sharding-report-strict` REQUIRES `--sharding-report`. Supplying
  it without `--sharding-report` is a usage error: exit `2`, exact
  stderr:

  ```
  ingest: --sharding-report-strict requires --sharding-report
  ```

- When enabled, the digest sidecar gains two extra fields,
  `sort_key` and `shards_sorted_invariant`, inserted between
  `shard_count` and `tool_version` (see §3). These two fields are the
  ONLY observable effect of `--sharding-report-strict`:
  - Report bytes are unchanged.
  - Index bytes are unchanged (index reads from the report and the
    report is strict-independent).
  - Histogram bytes are unchanged.
- Strict mode composes freely with `--sharding-report-sort`,
  `--sharding-report-digest`, `--sharding-report-index`, and
  `--sharding-report-histogram`.

## 2. `--sharding-report` file shape

The sharding-report file is pretty-printed JSON with **2-space
indentation** and a **trailing newline**. It is written via `.tmp` +
rename after all other outputs have been finalized. The top-level keys
appear in exactly this order:

```
schema_version, ingest_id, shards, totals
```

No other top-level keys are permitted.

- **`schema_version`**: a fixed string literal whose exact value is
  `"eventq.sharding-report/v1"`. Always the very first key; never
  `null`.
- **`ingest_id`**: when `--ingest-id <string>` was supplied to the
  current run, the JSON string of that value; otherwise JSON `null`.
  This mirrors the manifest feature's manifest `ingest_id` semantics exactly.
- **`shards`**: an array of per-shard records. By default (absent
  `--sharding-report-sort` or `--sharding-report-sort basename`) the
  order is **by basename in ASCII-ascending order** (the same order
  used by `--out-parquet-manifest`). Under `--sharding-report-sort
  bytes` the order is ascending by `bytes` with `shard` (basename) as
  tiebreaker. Under `--sharding-report-sort sha256` the order is
  ascending by `sha256` hex with `shard` as tiebreaker. The array MAY
  be empty (when `--input-dir` contains zero conforming shards).
  Every conforming shard contributes one entry, including shards with
  zero events (see §2.2).
- **`totals`**: an aggregate object over all shards (see §2.3).

### 2.1 Per-shard record keys

Each element of `shards` is a JSON object with exactly these keys in
this exact order:

```
shard, bytes, sha256,
events_total, events_accepted, events_invalid, events_late,
events_duplicate, events_unresolved_chargeback,
first_ts, last_ts
```

Semantics:

- `shard`: the bare basename of the shard file (e.g. `"a.ndjson"`).
- `bytes`: integer byte size of the shard file on disk (`wc -c`). Same
  as the manifest feature's manifest `input_shards[].bytes`.
- `sha256`: 64-char lowercase hex of the shard file's raw bytes on disk.
  Same as the manifest feature's manifest `input_shards[].sha256`. When the manifest is
  also emitted in the same run, these two sha256 values MUST match for
  every shard.
- `events_total`: number of non-blank lines in the shard (same
  definition as the manifest feature's manifest `input_shards[].events_total`). Blank /
  whitespace-only lines do NOT contribute.
- `events_accepted`: number of lines from this shard whose final
  audit-bucket is `accepted`. Same as the manifest feature's manifest
  `input_shards[].events_accepted`.
- `events_invalid`: number of lines from this shard whose final
  audit-bucket is `invalid` (schema-invalid, FX-invalid, amount-invalid,
  etc. — anything default base behavior counts toward the `invalid` reject bucket).
- `events_late`: number of lines from this shard whose final
  audit-bucket is `late` (dropped by the `--watermark-lag` rule).
- `events_duplicate`: number of lines from this shard whose final
  audit-bucket is `duplicate` under the dedup-policy dedup policy
  (either `first-arrival` or `lex-largest-ts`).
- `events_unresolved_chargeback`: number of lines from this shard whose
  final audit-bucket is `unresolved_chargeback` (chargeback with a
  `ref_id` that never resolved to a surviving purchase at end-of-run,
  per default base behavior).
- `first_ts`, `last_ts`: see §2.2.

### 2.2 `first_ts` / `last_ts` semantics (tricky)

For each shard, consider the lines that **parsed as JSON and have a
`ts` field whose value is an RFC 3339 UTC timestamp string** (of the
exact required UTC form `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ`) — regardless of whether the
event is ultimately `accepted`, `invalid`, `late`, `duplicate`, or
`unresolved_chargeback`. This is deliberately broader than
`--output.time_range` (which covers only accepted events) and broader
than `events_accepted` (which covers only the `accepted` bucket).

- A line that fails JSON parsing (e.g. `not a json object`) does NOT
  contribute to `first_ts` / `last_ts`.
- A line that parses as JSON but whose `ts` field is missing, empty, or
  not an RFC 3339 UTC string of the above exact form does NOT
  contribute.
- A line whose `ts` is well-formed but whose event is schema-invalid
  for another reason (e.g. missing `id`, bad `type`, negative `amount`)
  DOES contribute to `first_ts` / `last_ts`.

`first_ts` is the lexicographically smallest such `ts` string; `last_ts`
is the lexicographically largest. Because the fixed RFC 3339 format is
fixed-width and UTC-only, lexicographic order equals chronological
order. If no line in the shard contributes (either because
`events_total` is 0 or because every line fails parsing / lacks a valid
`ts`), BOTH `first_ts` and `last_ts` are JSON `null`.

A shard with `events_total == 0` therefore emits a record like:

```json
{
  "shard": "empty.ndjson",
  "bytes": 0,
  "sha256": "e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855",
  "events_total": 0,
  "events_accepted": 0,
  "events_invalid": 0,
  "events_late": 0,
  "events_duplicate": 0,
  "events_unresolved_chargeback": 0,
  "first_ts": null,
  "last_ts": null
}
```

(empty-file sha256 is fixed because it is the SHA-256 of the empty
byte string.)

### 2.3 Totals object

`totals` is a JSON object with exactly these keys in this exact order:

```
shards, bytes_total, events_total, events_accepted, events_invalid,
events_late, events_duplicate, events_unresolved_chargeback
```

- `shards`: integer count of entries in the `shards` array.
- `bytes_total`: sum of `bytes` across all shards.
- `events_total` through `events_unresolved_chargeback`: the
  element-wise sum across all shards' per-shard counts.

## 3. `--sharding-report-digest` file shape

The digest sidecar is pretty-printed JSON with **2-space indentation**
and a **trailing newline**. Written via `.tmp` + rename after the
sharding-report file has been written and renamed into place (so the
sha256 is computed on the finalized bytes). Top-level keys appear in
exactly this order:

```
schema_version, report_path, report_sha256, report_bytes, shard_count, tool_version
```

When `--sharding-report-strict` is supplied, the digest instead has
these keys in exactly this order (two extra fields inserted between
`shard_count` and `tool_version`):

```
schema_version, report_path, report_sha256, report_bytes, shard_count,
sort_key, shards_sorted_invariant, tool_version
```

No other top-level keys are permitted in either mode.

- `schema_version`: fixed string literal
  `"eventq.sharding-report-digest/v1"`.
- `report_path`: the bare basename of the sharding-report file (e.g.
  `"shards.json"`) — NOT the full path supplied to `--sharding-report`.
- `report_sha256`: 64-char lowercase hex sha256 of the sharding-report
  file's on-disk bytes (the `.json` file the digest describes).
- `report_bytes`: integer byte size of the sharding-report file on disk.
- `shard_count`: integer count of entries in the sharding-report's
  `shards` array (equal to `totals.shards`).
- `sort_key` (strict only): JSON string echoing the
  `--sharding-report-sort` value actually used. One of `"basename"`,
  `"bytes"`, or `"sha256"`. When `--sharding-report-sort` is absent
  but `--sharding-report-strict` is supplied, the value is the
  default `"basename"`.
- `shards_sorted_invariant` (strict only): JSON boolean. `true` when
  the sharding-report's `shards[]` array is in the order required by
  `sort_key` (including the basename tiebreaker for `bytes`/`sha256`);
  `false` otherwise. A conforming producer always emits `true`.
- `tool_version`: fixed string literal `"eventq/0.5.0"`.

The digest is deterministic: identical inputs and identical flag values
produce byte-identical digest files. When `--sharding-report-strict`
is absent AND `--sharding-report-sort` is absent or explicitly
`basename`, the digest bytes are byte-identical to the default
basename-sorted state. Under `--sharding-report-sort bytes` or `sha256`,
the digest remains deterministic but its bytes differ because
`report_sha256` (and therefore the serialized digest payload) changes
with the reordered report bytes.

## 4. `--sharding-report-index` file shape

The index sidecar is pretty-printed JSON with **2-space indentation**
and a **trailing newline**. Written via `.tmp` + rename after the
digest has been written (so it observes the finalized report + digest
bytes). Top-level keys appear in exactly this order:

```
entries, entry_count, shard_count, generated_from, tool_version
```

No other top-level keys are permitted.

- `entries`: an ordered JSON array. For each shard in the
  sharding-report's `shards` array (in the same order as that report's
  `shards[]` array, whether basename-sorted or reordered by
  `--sharding-report-sort`), the index emits ONE `{name, type, path}`
  entry per per-shard scalar field, in exactly this key order within
  each shard:

  ```
  shard, bytes, sha256,
  events_total, events_accepted, events_invalid, events_late,
  events_duplicate, events_unresolved_chargeback,
  first_ts, last_ts
  ```

  Each entry has exactly these keys in this exact order:

  ```
  name, type, path
  ```

  - `name`: the per-shard field name (e.g. `"bytes"`, `"first_ts"`).
  - `type`: one of `"string"`, `"integer"`, or `"null"`, classified
    from the actual value in the sharding-report for that shard:
    - JSON strings → `"string"`.
    - JSON integers → `"integer"`.
    - JSON null → `"null"` (applies to `first_ts` / `last_ts` when
      they are null for that shard).
  - `path`: JSONPath of the form `$.shards[<i>].<name>`, where `<i>` is
    the 0-based position of the shard in the sharding-report's
    `shards` array. Example: `"$.shards[0].bytes"`.

  When the sharding-report's `shards` array is empty, `entries` is
  the empty array `[]`.
- `entry_count`: integer count of entries in the `entries` array.
  Equal to `11 * shard_count` (exactly 11 per-shard fields per shard).
- `shard_count`: integer count of shards covered (equal to
  sharding-report's `totals.shards`).
- `generated_from`: the bare basename of the sharding-report file
  (e.g. `"shards.json"`) — same value as the digest's `report_path`.
- `tool_version`: fixed string literal `"eventq/0.5.0"`.

The index is deterministic.

## 4.5 `--sharding-report-histogram` file shape

The histogram sidecar is pretty-printed JSON with **2-space
indentation** and a **trailing newline**. Written via `.tmp` + rename
after the sharding-report has been written. (The digest and index
sidecars are NOT required; the histogram is independent of them.)
Top-level keys appear in exactly this order:

```
schema_version, report_path, shard_count, buckets, tool_version
```

No other top-level keys are permitted.

- `schema_version`: fixed string literal
  `"eventq.sharding-report-histogram/v1"`.
- `report_path`: the bare basename of the sharding-report file (e.g.
  `"shards.json"`) — NOT the full path supplied to `--sharding-report`.
- `shard_count`: integer count of shards covered (equal to
  sharding-report's `totals.shards`).
- `buckets`: a JSON object with exactly these keys in exactly this
  order, where each value is a histogram array over the named
  per-shard scalar metric computed across all shards in the
  sharding-report:

  ```
  bytes, events_total, events_accepted, events_invalid,
  events_late, events_duplicate, events_unresolved_chargeback
  ```

- `tool_version`: fixed string literal `"eventq/0.5.0"`.

### 4.5.1 Histogram array semantics (deterministic)

For a given metric M (e.g. `bytes` or `events_accepted`), let
`values[]` be the non-negative integer vector consisting of one entry
per shard in the sharding-report's `shards` array, in whatever order
that report currently uses (default `basename`, or the reordered
`bytes` / `sha256` order selected by `--sharding-report-sort`). Entry
`i` is `shards[i].<M>` for the per-shard scalar fields `bytes`,
`events_total`, `events_accepted`, `events_invalid`, `events_late`,
`events_duplicate`, `events_unresolved_chargeback`. Because the
histogram reduces `values[]` to bucket counts, its output depends only
on the multiset of metric values, not on the enumeration order.

The histogram array for metric M is constructed deterministically:

1. If the `shards` array is empty, the histogram array is the empty
   array `[]`.
2. Otherwise let `vmax = max(values[])`.
3. If `vmax == 0`, the histogram array is exactly one bucket:
   `[{"upper_bound": 0, "count": len(values)}]`. Every shard
   contributes to the single zero-bucket.
4. If `vmax >= 1`, the histogram is a sequence of power-of-two
   buckets starting at upper_bound `1` and doubling until
   `upper_bound >= vmax`. Concretely the upper-bounds are
   `1, 2, 4, 8, 16, ...` truncated at the smallest power of two
   greater than or equal to `vmax`. A separate leading zero-bucket
   `{"upper_bound": 0, "count": N0}` is emitted **only if** at least
   one shard has `values[i] == 0`, where `N0` is the count of shards
   with `values[i] == 0`. When emitted, the zero-bucket is the first
   element of the histogram array; the power-of-two buckets follow.
5. For each power-of-two bucket with upper_bound `u`, the `count`
   field is the number of shards whose `values[i]` satisfies
   `prev < values[i] <= u`, where `prev` is the previous bucket's
   upper_bound (treat `prev = 0` for the first power-of-two bucket,
   regardless of whether the zero-bucket was emitted).
6. Each bucket is a JSON object with exactly these keys in exactly
   this order:

   ```
   upper_bound, count
   ```

   Both are non-negative integers.

### 4.5.2 Worked example

If the sharding-report has two shards with
`events_accepted = [1, 3]`, then `vmax = 3`, power-of-two
upper_bounds are `1, 2, 4`. Counts: `prev=0, u=1 → count=1` (value 1);
`prev=1, u=2 → count=0`; `prev=2, u=4 → count=1` (value 3). No shard
has value 0, so no leading zero-bucket. Histogram:

```json
[
  {"upper_bound": 1, "count": 1},
  {"upper_bound": 2, "count": 0},
  {"upper_bound": 4, "count": 1}
]
```

If `events_late = [0, 0]` for every shard, histogram is exactly
`[{"upper_bound": 0, "count": 2}]`.

If `events_invalid = [3, 0]`, then `vmax = 3`, zero-bucket is
emitted (N0=1), and power-of-two buckets run 1, 2, 4. Counts:
zero-bucket 1; `u=1 → 0`; `u=2 → 0`; `u=4 → 1`. Histogram:

```json
[
  {"upper_bound": 0, "count": 1},
  {"upper_bound": 1, "count": 0},
  {"upper_bound": 2, "count": 0},
  {"upper_bound": 4, "count": 1}
]
```

### 4.5.3 Invariants

For every histogram array `H` emitted by `--sharding-report-histogram`
(one per named metric in `buckets`):

- If `shard_count == 0`, `H == []`.
- Otherwise `sum(bucket.count for bucket in H) == shard_count`
  (every shard contributes to exactly one bucket).
- `bucket.upper_bound` values are strictly increasing along `H`.
- `bucket.upper_bound >= 0` and `bucket.count >= 0`.
- When a zero-bucket is present, it is the first element and has
  `upper_bound == 0`.
- All non-zero buckets have `upper_bound` equal to a power of two
  (`1, 2, 4, 8, ...`).

The histogram sidecar is deterministic: identical inputs and
identical flag values produce byte-identical histogram files. The
presence or absence of `--sharding-report-digest` and
`--sharding-report-index` does NOT affect the histogram bytes.
Likewise, the histogram does NOT affect the sharding-report, digest,
or index bytes.

## 5. Per-shard invariants (strict)

For every shard record in the sharding-report:

1. `events_total >= 0`, `bytes >= 0`, every `events_*` count `>= 0`.
2. `events_accepted + events_invalid + events_late + events_duplicate + events_unresolved_chargeback == events_total`.
3. `first_ts == null` iff `last_ts == null`. Either both are null or
   both are non-null.
4. When both are non-null, `first_ts <= last_ts` (string comparison).
5. When the run also emits `--out-parquet-manifest`, for every shard
   that appears in both files:
   - `bytes` matches the manifest's `input_shards[].bytes`.
   - `sha256` matches the manifest's `input_shards[].sha256`.
   - `events_total` matches the manifest's
     `input_shards[].events_total`.
   - `events_accepted` matches the manifest's
     `input_shards[].events_accepted`.

## 6. Top-level invariants (strict)

1. `totals.shards == len(shards)`.
2. `totals.bytes_total == sum(s.bytes for s in shards)`.
3. `totals.events_total == sum(s.events_total for s in shards)`.
4. For each bucket in `{accepted, invalid, late, duplicate,
   unresolved_chargeback}`:
   `totals.events_<bucket> == sum(s.events_<bucket> for s in shards)`.
5. When the run also emits `--out-parquet-manifest` and
   `manifest.totals.events_accepted_total` / `.events_rejected_total`
   exist:
   - `totals.events_accepted == manifest.totals.events_accepted_total`.
   - `totals.events_invalid + totals.events_late +
      totals.events_duplicate + totals.events_unresolved_chargeback
      == manifest.totals.events_rejected_total`.
6. The sharding-report, digest, and index are produced
   **deterministically**: repeated runs with identical inputs and
   identical flag values produce byte-identical sidecar files.

## 7. Composition with other flags

- The six new flags do NOT change any existing output on any
  input. `--output`, `--rejects`, `--state-out`, `--audit-log`,
  `--audit-log-digest`, `--out-parquet-manifest`,
  `--manifest-signature`, `--summary-schema-out`,
  `--summary-schema-digest`, `--summary-schema-index` are byte-identical
  to a run with all six new flags omitted (everything else equal).
- `--sharding-report-sort basename` (explicit) and `--sharding-report-sort`
  absent produce byte-identical report, digest, index, and histogram
  bytes (when `--sharding-report-strict` is absent). Strict is purely
  additive to the digest.
- Under `--sharding-report-sort bytes` or `sha256`, only the
  sharding-report bytes and the digest's `report_sha256`/`report_bytes`
  change (plus `--sharding-report-index` JSONPath indices reflect the
  new order). Histogram bytes do NOT depend on sort key.
- Supplying `--sharding-report-digest`, `--sharding-report-index`,
  and/or `--sharding-report-histogram` does NOT change the
  sharding-report bytes either. The sharding-report is byte-identical
  whether or not the digest/index/histogram flags are present.
- `--sharding-report-histogram` is independent of
  `--sharding-report-digest` and `--sharding-report-index`: histogram
  bytes do not depend on either, and neither of those sidecars is
  affected by the histogram flag. Any subset of
  `{digest, index, histogram}` may be emitted, provided the
  `--sharding-report-index` requires-both rule is satisfied.
- Under `--session-policy fixed-window` or `--session-policy hybrid`,
  the sharding-report's per-shard and total event counts are
  **byte-identical** to a `--session-policy gap` run on the same
  input, because the session-policy feature's policy flag affects only
  `summary.sessions.total` (a summary-scoped integer) and does not
  reclassify any event's audit-bucket.
- Under `--dedup-policy lex-largest-ts` (the dedup-policy feature), a line's winner/loser
  status under the lex rule determines whether the shard containing
  that line contributes to `events_accepted` vs `events_duplicate`.
  Winning and losing lines from different shards are attributed to
  their own shards independently.
- Under `--rejects-format tagged` (the rejects-format feature), the sharding-report is
  unchanged — it is not a rejects projection; the per-bucket counts
  are derived from audit outcomes, not from `--rejects` bytes.

## 8. Determinism

Given identical `--input-dir`, `--rates`, `--session-gap`,
`--watermark-lag`, any existing flag combination, and any
`--sharding-report <path>` / `--sharding-report-digest <path>` /
`--sharding-report-index <path>` / `--sharding-report-histogram <path>` /
`--sharding-report-sort <key>` / `--sharding-report-strict` values,
the on-disk bytes of each produced sidecar are byte-identical across
repeated runs. No wall clock, no hostname, no environment variable
contributes.

## 9. Usage

```
eventq ingest --input-dir <dir> --rates <path> --output <path> --rejects <path> \
              --session-gap <seconds> --watermark-lag <seconds> \
              [--state-in <path>] [--state-out <path>] [--pending-ttl <seconds>] \
              [--max-open-sessions <int>] [--state-strict] \
              [--audit-log <path>] [--audit-log-digest <path>] \
              [--audit-log-rotate-bytes <int>] \
              [--rates-strict] [--rates-max-staleness <seconds>] \
              [--ingest-id <string>] [--out-parquet-manifest <path>] \
              [--manifest-signature <path>] \
              [--dedup-policy <first-arrival|lex-largest-ts>] \
              [--strict-late-audit] \
              [--summary-schema-out <path>] \
              [--summary-schema-digest <path>] \
              [--summary-schema-strict] \
              [--summary-schema-index <path>] \
              [--rejects-format <legacy|tagged|tagged-sorted>] \
              [--rejects-format-header] \
              [--session-policy <gap|fixed-window|hybrid>] \
              [--fixed-window-seconds <int>] \
              [--sharding-report <path>] \
              [--sharding-report-digest <path>] \
              [--sharding-report-index <path>] \
              [--sharding-report-histogram <path>] \
              [--sharding-report-sort <basename|bytes|sha256>] \
              [--sharding-report-strict]
```

rounds.title: Run Provenance Sidecars
rounds.md:
# Run Provenance Sidecars

Add a standalone run-provenance sidecar so operators can see which
`eventq ingest` flag values were observed on the command line and how the set
of input shards was fingerprinted. The new sidecar avoids reconstructing that
information from logs, state files, manifests, and other sidecars.

`eventq ingest` now accepts exactly three new optional flags:
`--run-provenance`, `--run-provenance-digest`, and
`--run-provenance-strict`. When all three flags are absent, every existing
output remains byte-for-byte unchanged. When any of the three flags are
supplied, existing outputs remain identical to a run without them; only the
new provenance sidecar, its digest, or the strict-mode provenance keys appear.

## 1. Flag syntax

```
eventq ingest ... [--run-provenance <path>]
                   [--run-provenance-digest <path>]
                   [--run-provenance-strict]
```

### 1.1 `--run-provenance <path>`

- `--run-provenance` is **optional** and takes a **single string value**
  (a filesystem path). It is absent by default.
- When `--run-provenance` is absent, the CLI does NOT create the
  file at any path; no `--run-provenance` byte stream exists on disk,
  and every existing output remains byte-identical to the default run.
- When `--run-provenance` is supplied, the value MUST be a non-empty
  string. An empty value (`--run-provenance=` or
  `--run-provenance ""`) is a usage error: exit code `2`, no output
  files created (neither `--output` nor `--rejects` nor any sidecar),
  and this exact stderr line:

  ```
  ingest: --run-provenance: value must be non-empty
  ```

- The parent directory of the supplied `--run-provenance` path MUST
  already exist. If it does not exist, exit `2`, no output files
  created, stderr (exact):

  ```
  ingest --run-provenance parent directory does not exist
  ```

- `--run-provenance` composes freely with every existing flag. In
  particular it is NOT gated on `--out-parquet-manifest`,
  `--audit-log`, `--summary-schema-out`, `--sharding-report`, etc.
  A run that passes only
  `--input-dir/--rates/--output/--rejects/--session-gap/--watermark-lag/--run-provenance`
  is valid.
- Validation order: the empty-value check runs at argument-parse time;
  the parent-directory-exists check runs **before** any output file is
  written (including any `--output.tmp` or `--rejects.tmp`).

### 1.2 `--run-provenance-digest <path>`

- `--run-provenance-digest` is **optional** and takes a **single string
  value** (a filesystem path). It is absent by default.
- When absent, the CLI does NOT create the digest file at any path.
- When supplied, the value MUST be a non-empty string. An empty value
  is a usage error: exit code `2`, no output files created, exact
  stderr line:

  ```
  ingest: --run-provenance-digest: value must be non-empty
  ```

- `--run-provenance-digest` REQUIRES `--run-provenance`. Supplying
  `--run-provenance-digest` without `--run-provenance` is a usage
  error: exit code `2`, no output files created, exact stderr line:

  ```
  ingest: --run-provenance-digest requires --run-provenance
  ```

- The parent directory of the supplied path MUST already exist. If it
  does not, exit `2`, no output files created, exact stderr line:

  ```
  ingest --run-provenance-digest parent directory does not exist
  ```

- Validation order: empty-value check → requires-run-provenance check
  → parent-directory-exists check. All three run BEFORE any output
  file is written.

### 1.3 `--run-provenance-strict`

- `--run-provenance-strict` is a **boolean** flag and is absent by
  default. It takes no value; bare `--run-provenance-strict` records
  as `true`, `--run-provenance-strict=false` records as `false`
  (identical convention to `--state-strict`, `--rates-strict`,
  `--sharding-report-strict`).
- `--run-provenance-strict` REQUIRES `--run-provenance`. Supplying
  `--run-provenance-strict` without `--run-provenance` is a usage
  error: exit code `2`, no output files created (neither `--output`
  nor `--rejects` nor any sidecar), this exact stderr line:

  ```
  ingest: --run-provenance-strict requires --run-provenance
  ```

- Under `--run-provenance-strict`, an empty input directory (zero
  conforming shards) is a usage error: exit code `2`, no output files
  created, this exact stderr line:

  ```
  ingest --run-provenance-strict: input-dir has no conforming shards
  ```

  Without `--run-provenance-strict` an empty input directory continues
  to be accepted (`shard_count == 0` with the fixed empty-fingerprint
  literal), unchanged from the default non-strict provenance output.
- Validation order: requires-run-provenance check (fires at argument-
  parse time) → empty-input-dir check (fires right after the
  provenance parent-directory check and BEFORE any output file is
  written).
- `--run-provenance-strict=false` (explicitly disabled) behaves
  identically to `--run-provenance-strict` being absent: no extra
  keys, no empty-input-dir rejection. The flag is recorded in
  `flags["run-provenance-strict"]` either way (observed via
  `fs.Visit`).

## 2. `--run-provenance` file shape

The run-provenance file is pretty-printed JSON with **2-space
indentation** and a **trailing newline**. It is written via `.tmp` +
rename after all other outputs (including every existing sidecar) have
been finalized. Top-level keys appear in exactly this order:

```
schema_version, tool_version, flags, inputs_fingerprint, shard_count
```

When `--run-provenance-strict` is explicitly set to `true` (bare
`--run-provenance-strict` or `--run-provenance-strict=true`), the
top-level key list is extended IN ORDER by two additional keys
immediately after `shard_count`:

```
schema_version, tool_version, flags, inputs_fingerprint, shard_count,
argv_sha256, env_fingerprint
```

The `argv_sha256` and `env_fingerprint` keys are present ONLY when
strict mode is explicitly enabled (true). In every other case —
`--run-provenance-strict` absent, or explicitly `false` — the
top-level key list is the 5-key default. No other top-level keys are
permitted.

- **`schema_version`**: a fixed string literal whose exact value is
  `"eventq.run-provenance/v1"`. Always the very first key; never
  `null`.
- **`tool_version`**: fixed string literal `"eventq/0.5.0"`. Identical
  to the `tool_version` field emitted by existing digests/sidecars.
- **`flags`**: a JSON object; see §2.1.
- **`inputs_fingerprint`**: a 64-char lowercase-hex sha256 string; see
  §2.2. When `shard_count == 0`, the value is the sha256 of the
  empty byte string
  (`"e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855"`).
- **`shard_count`**: integer count of conforming shards observed in
  `--input-dir`. Same value as
  `--out-parquet-manifest.input_shards[]` length and
  `--sharding-report.totals.shards` on the same inputs. `>= 0`.

### 2.1 `flags` object

The `flags` object enumerates each `eventq ingest` flag observed via
`fs.Visit` (i.e. **explicitly set on the command line**, not merely
holding a default). Keys are the flag names **without the leading
`--`**, in a fixed enumeration order (see §2.3). Values are JSON
encodings of the observed values.

Value encoding rules (deterministic):

- **String-valued flags** (e.g. `--input-dir`, `--rates`, `--output`,
  `--rejects`, `--state-in`, `--state-out`, `--audit-log`,
  `--audit-log-digest`, `--ingest-id`, `--out-parquet-manifest`,
  `--manifest-signature`, `--dedup-policy`, `--summary-schema-out`,
  `--summary-schema-digest`, `--summary-schema-index`,
  `--rejects-format`, `--session-policy`, `--sharding-report`,
  `--sharding-report-digest`, `--sharding-report-index`,
  `--sharding-report-histogram`, `--sharding-report-sort`,
  `--run-provenance`, `--run-provenance-digest`) →
  JSON strings. File-path-valued flags record the value **as observed**
  on the command line (i.e. the raw argv token). No resolution /
  canonicalization.
- **Integer-valued flags** (e.g. `--session-gap`, `--watermark-lag`,
  `--pending-ttl`, `--max-open-sessions`,
  `--audit-log-rotate-bytes`, `--rates-max-staleness`,
  `--fixed-window-seconds`) → JSON integers.
- **Boolean-valued flags** (e.g. `--state-strict`, `--rates-strict`,
  `--strict-late-audit`, `--summary-schema-strict`,
  `--rejects-format-header`, `--sharding-report-strict`,
  `--run-provenance-strict`) → JSON booleans. A boolean flag passed
  without a value (the standard Go `flag` package convention for bare
  `--state-strict`) records as `true`. `--state-strict=false` records
  as `false`.

Flags that are NOT explicitly set are OMITTED from the `flags`
object — they do NOT appear with `null`, and they do NOT appear with
their default. The object has exactly one key per observed flag.

### 2.2 `inputs_fingerprint` semantics (fixed)

Let `shards` be the list of conforming shard files under
`--input-dir` in **ASCII-ascending basename order** (same order as
the manifest feature's manifest `input_shards` and the sharding-report feature's default sharding-report
ordering). For each shard compute `sha256_hex(shard_bytes)` as a
64-char lowercase hex string. Let `concat` be the byte string formed
by concatenating those hex strings in order (no separator; no
newline). `inputs_fingerprint` is `sha256_hex(concat)`.

When `shards == []`, `concat` is the empty byte string and
`inputs_fingerprint ==
"e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855"`.

`inputs_fingerprint` is independent of `--sharding-report-sort`: even
when the sharding-report's `shards[]` is reordered by bytes/sha256,
`inputs_fingerprint` uses the basename-ascending order of the input
directory.

### 2.3 `flags` enumeration order (fixed)

When multiple flags are explicitly set, the `flags` object MUST list
them in exactly this sequence (only the keys that were actually
explicitly set appear; all others are omitted):

```
input-dir, rates, output, rejects, session-gap, watermark-lag,
state-in, state-out, pending-ttl, max-open-sessions, state-strict,
audit-log, audit-log-digest, audit-log-rotate-bytes,
rates-strict, rates-max-staleness,
ingest-id, out-parquet-manifest, manifest-signature,
dedup-policy, strict-late-audit,
summary-schema-out, summary-schema-digest, summary-schema-strict, summary-schema-index,
rejects-format, rejects-format-header,
session-policy, fixed-window-seconds,
sharding-report, sharding-report-digest, sharding-report-index,
sharding-report-histogram, sharding-report-sort, sharding-report-strict,
run-provenance, run-provenance-digest, run-provenance-strict
```

The order does NOT depend on the order the flags were typed on the
command line; it is a fixed enumeration so the sidecar bytes are
reproducible across harness invocations.

### 2.4 `argv_sha256` (strict mode only)

`argv_sha256` is a 64-char lowercase-hex sha256 string binding the
run-provenance file to the argv that produced it, while remaining
independent of absolute filesystem path values.

- Input tokens: `os.Args[1:]` (every argv token except the program
  name).
- Filter: drop every token whose raw bytes contain a forward slash
  (`/`). This removes path-valued flag values (e.g. `--input-dir
  /tmp/in_multi` collapses to just `--input-dir`) and any argv token
  that looks like a filesystem path. Flag names themselves (which
  start with `--` but contain no `/`) are retained.
- Join: remaining tokens joined by a single ASCII space with no
  leading/trailing separators and no trailing newline.
- Hash: `sha256_hex(joined)` where `joined` is the UTF-8 bytes of the
  joined string.

When every argv token is filtered out (pathological case — no non-
path tokens at all), `joined` is the empty string and `argv_sha256`
is the fixed sha256-of-empty literal
`"e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855"`.

`argv_sha256` is stable across changes to path values (e.g. moving
the input-dir from `/tmp/a` to `/tmp/b`) and across different working
directories, provided the non-path tokens and their order are
unchanged. Reordering argv DOES change `argv_sha256`.

### 2.5 `env_fingerprint` (strict mode only)

`env_fingerprint` is a 64-char lowercase-hex sha256 string binding
the run to the set of `EVENTQ_*` environment variable **names**
present in the process environment, with values redacted.

- Input: the process environment at the moment the ingest invocation
  begins. Iterate `os.Environ()`; retain every entry whose `NAME=...`
  prefix begins with `EVENTQ_` (ASCII-literal prefix, case-
  sensitive).
- Redact: strip values entirely. Only the variable **name** is used.
- Sort: ASCII-ascending by name.
- Join: each retained name followed by a single LF (`\n`), in sorted
  order. No leading LF. The final byte is always LF when at least one
  name is retained; when zero names are retained, the joined byte
  string is empty.
- Hash: `sha256_hex(joined)`.

When no `EVENTQ_*` variable is set (the common case), `env_fingerprint`
is the fixed sha256-of-empty literal
`"e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855"`.

`env_fingerprint` is deliberately redacted: secret values that might
leak via `EVENTQ_API_KEY=...` do NOT contribute to the hash; only the
name-set does. Two runs with identical `EVENTQ_*` name-sets and
different values produce identical `env_fingerprint` bytes.

## 3. `--run-provenance-digest` file shape

The digest sidecar is pretty-printed JSON with **2-space indentation**
and a **trailing newline**. Written via `.tmp` + rename after the
run-provenance file has been written and renamed into place (so the
sha256 is computed on the finalized bytes). Top-level keys appear in
exactly this order:

```
schema_version, provenance_path, provenance_sha256, provenance_bytes, tool_version
```

No other top-level keys are permitted.

- `schema_version`: fixed string literal
  `"eventq.run-provenance-digest/v1"`.
- `provenance_path`: the **bare basename** of the run-provenance file
  (e.g. `"provenance.json"`) — NOT the full path supplied to
  `--run-provenance`.
- `provenance_sha256`: 64-char lowercase hex sha256 of the
  run-provenance file's on-disk bytes (the `.json` file the digest
  describes).
- `provenance_bytes`: integer byte size of the run-provenance file on
  disk.
- `tool_version`: fixed string literal `"eventq/0.5.0"`.

The digest is deterministic: identical inputs and identical flag
values produce byte-identical digest files.

## 4. Invariants (strict)

1. Given identical `--input-dir` contents, identical `--rates` file
   bytes, and identical explicit flag set + values, the
   `--run-provenance` file bytes are byte-identical across repeated
   runs. No wall clock, no hostname, no environment variable
   contributes.
2. `inputs_fingerprint` is a pure function of the input-dir shards
   (their names and bytes); it does NOT depend on any flag value,
   including `--sharding-report-sort`.
3. When `--run-provenance-digest` is supplied, its
   `provenance_sha256` equals `sha256(provenance_file_bytes)` and
   `provenance_bytes` equals `wc -c provenance_file`.
4. The presence or absence of `--run-provenance-digest` does NOT
   change any derived run facts in `--run-provenance`
   (`inputs_fingerprint`, `shard_count`, and, when strict is enabled,
   `argv_sha256` / `env_fingerprint`). Its only effect on the
   `--run-provenance` payload is that `flags["run-provenance-digest"]`
   appears when that flag was explicitly set.
5. The presence of `--run-provenance` / `--run-provenance-digest`
   does NOT change any existing output bytes. All existing sidecars are
   byte-identical to a run with both new flags omitted (everything
   else equal).
6. The `flags` object's key set equals the set of flags observed via
   `fs.Visit` on the current `ingest` invocation. Flags holding
   their default value but NOT passed on argv do NOT appear.
7. `flags["run-provenance"]` IS included whenever
   `--run-provenance` was explicitly set (and it always is when the
   sidecar is emitted); its value is the observed argv token.
   Likewise `flags["run-provenance-digest"]` is included when that
   flag is set.
8. The `flags` object's key iteration order equals the fixed
   enumeration order of §2.3, independent of argv ordering.
9. When `--run-provenance-strict` is NOT explicitly set (absent), the
   on-disk bytes of the `--run-provenance` file are byte-identical to
   the default non-strict provenance output (no `argv_sha256`, no `env_fingerprint`;
   no `"run-provenance-strict"` key inside `flags`). Adding
   `--run-provenance-strict=false` appears inside `flags` but does
   NOT add the two extra top-level keys.
10. `argv_sha256` is invariant under changes to path-valued flag
    values (tokens containing `/`): two runs with identical non-path
    argv tokens but differing `--input-dir` / `--output` / `--rejects`
    path values produce the same `argv_sha256`.
11. `env_fingerprint` is invariant under changes to `EVENTQ_*`
    environment variable **values**: only the name-set contributes.
    Two runs with identical `EVENTQ_*` name-sets and different values
    produce the same `env_fingerprint`.
12. Under `--run-provenance-strict`, an input directory containing
    zero conforming shards is a usage error (exit 2, no output
    files). Without `--run-provenance-strict` the same input
    directory continues to produce `shard_count == 0` and the fixed
    empty-fingerprint literal, unchanged from the default non-strict output.

## 5. Composition with other flags

- The three new flags do NOT change any existing output on any
  input. `--output`, `--rejects`, `--state-out`, `--audit-log`,
  `--audit-log-digest`, `--out-parquet-manifest`,
  `--manifest-signature`, `--summary-schema-out`,
  `--summary-schema-digest`, `--summary-schema-index`,
  `--sharding-report`, `--sharding-report-digest`,
  `--sharding-report-index`, `--sharding-report-histogram` are
  byte-identical to a run with all three new flags omitted.
- Under `--sharding-report-sort bytes` / `sha256`, the
  `--run-provenance` `inputs_fingerprint` is UNCHANGED — it always
  uses basename-ascending order of `--input-dir` shards.
- Under `--session-policy fixed-window` / `hybrid` the provenance
  file records `flags["session-policy"]` (and
  `flags["fixed-window-seconds"]` if set), but `inputs_fingerprint`
  and `shard_count` are unaffected by the policy.
- Under `--dedup-policy lex-largest-ts` the provenance file records
  `flags["dedup-policy"]`, but `inputs_fingerprint` and
  `shard_count` are unaffected.

## 6. Determinism

Given identical `--input-dir` contents, identical `--rates` bytes,
and identical explicit flag set + values, the on-disk bytes of each
produced sidecar are byte-identical across repeated runs. No wall
clock or hostname contributes. Environment only contributes when
`--run-provenance-strict=true`, and then only through the
`EVENTQ_*` name-set encoded into `env_fingerprint` per §2.5; variable
values themselves do not contribute.

## 7. Usage

```
eventq ingest --input-dir <dir> --rates <path> --output <path> --rejects <path> \
              --session-gap <seconds> --watermark-lag <seconds> \
              [--state-in <path>] [--state-out <path>] [--pending-ttl <seconds>] \
              [--max-open-sessions <int>] [--state-strict] \
              [--audit-log <path>] [--audit-log-digest <path>] \
              [--audit-log-rotate-bytes <int>] \
              [--rates-strict] [--rates-max-staleness <seconds>] \
              [--ingest-id <string>] [--out-parquet-manifest <path>] \
              [--manifest-signature <path>] \
              [--dedup-policy <first-arrival|lex-largest-ts>] \
              [--strict-late-audit] \
              [--summary-schema-out <path>] \
              [--summary-schema-digest <path>] \
              [--summary-schema-strict] \
              [--summary-schema-index <path>] \
              [--rejects-format <legacy|tagged|tagged-sorted>] \
              [--rejects-format-header] \
              [--session-policy <gap|fixed-window|hybrid>] \
              [--fixed-window-seconds <int>] \
              [--sharding-report <path>] \
              [--sharding-report-digest <path>] \
              [--sharding-report-index <path>] \
              [--sharding-report-histogram <path>] \
              [--sharding-report-sort <basename|bytes|sha256>] \
              [--sharding-report-strict] \
              [--run-provenance <path>] \
              [--run-provenance-digest <path>] \
              [--run-provenance-strict]
```

rounds.title: Summary Time Range Policy
rounds.md:
# Summary Time Range Policy

`summary.time_range` currently reflects accepted events only, while the
sharding report's `first_ts` and `last_ts` fields reflect every JSON-parseable
line with a conforming UTC timestamp, even if the event is later rejected. Add
`--summary-time-range-policy <accepted|observed|strict-observed>` so operators
can keep the accepted-only behavior, opt into the broader observed range, or
enforce that the accepted and observed ranges coincide.

The default `accepted` policy preserves the existing `summary.time_range`
bytes. Under `observed`, `time_range.min` and `time_range.max` in `--output`
are recomputed over the same timestamp corpus used by the sharding report.
Under `strict-observed`, the run succeeds only when the accepted-only and
observed ranges are identical. No new output files, top-level keys, or renamed
keys are introduced.

## 1. Flag syntax

```
eventq ingest ... [--summary-time-range-policy <accepted|observed|strict-observed>]
```

### 1.1 `--summary-time-range-policy <value>`

- `--summary-time-range-policy` is **optional** and takes a **single
  string value**. It is absent by default.
- When `--summary-time-range-policy` is absent, it behaves exactly as
  `--summary-time-range-policy accepted`: the emitted
  `summary.time_range` in `--output` is byte-identical to the existing
  accepted-only behavior, and the observation that the flag
  was not passed is NOT reflected in any sidecar.
- When `--summary-time-range-policy` is supplied, the value MUST be a
  non-empty string. An empty value (`--summary-time-range-policy=` or
  `--summary-time-range-policy ""`) is a usage error: exit code `2`,
  no output files created (neither `--output` nor `--rejects` nor any
  sidecar), and this exact stderr line:

  ```
  ingest: --summary-time-range-policy: value must be non-empty
  ```

- When `--summary-time-range-policy` is supplied, the value MUST be
  one of the exact ASCII literals `accepted`, `observed`, or
  `strict-observed` (lower-case, no surrounding whitespace). Any other
  value is a usage error: exit code `2`, no output files created,
  exact stderr line:

  ```
  ingest: --summary-time-range-policy: value must be one of accepted|observed|strict-observed
  ```

- Validation order: the empty-value check runs at argument-parse time;
  the enumeration-membership check runs immediately after. Both run
  BEFORE any output file is written (including any `--output.tmp` or
  `--rejects.tmp`).

- `--summary-time-range-policy` composes freely with every existing
  flag. In particular it is NOT gated on `--sharding-report`,
  `--summary-schema-out`, `--run-provenance`, etc. It applies purely
  to the `time_range` block of `--output`.

## 2. `accepted` semantics (default; byte-identity preservation)

When `--summary-time-range-policy` is absent or `accepted`:

- The `time_range` block emitted inside `--output` is byte-identical
  to the existing accepted-only behavior. The `time_range.min` field is the
  lexicographically smallest `ts` among the **surviving / accepted**
  events that end up in `summary.count_by_type` (i.e. the events
  written to the accepted corpus by base ingest semantics). The
  `time_range.max` field is the lexicographically largest such `ts`.
- When zero events are accepted, both `time_range.min` and
  `time_range.max` are JSON `null` (unchanged from default base behavior).

## 3. `observed` semantics (opt-in correction)

When `--summary-time-range-policy observed` is supplied:

- The corpus for `time_range.min` / `time_range.max` is **broader**:
  it covers every input line that (a) parsed as a JSON object, AND
  (b) contains a `ts` field whose value is an RFC 3339 UTC timestamp
  of the exact required UTC form `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ` — regardless of
  which audit bucket the line ultimately landed in (`accepted`,
  `invalid`, `late`, `duplicate`, or `unresolved_chargeback`).
- A line that fails JSON parsing (e.g. `not a json object`) does NOT
  contribute.
- A line that parses as JSON but whose `ts` field is missing, empty,
  or not an RFC 3339 UTC string of the exact form does NOT
  contribute.
- A line whose `ts` is well-formed but whose event is schema-invalid
  for another reason (e.g. missing `id`, bad `type`, negative
  `amount`) DOES contribute.
- `time_range.min` is the lexicographically smallest such `ts` across
  every shard's contributing lines in `--input-dir`; `time_range.max`
  is the lexicographically largest. Because the fixed RFC 3339 form
  is fixed-width and UTC-only, lexicographic order equals chronological
  order.
- When zero lines across the entire input directory contribute to the
  broader corpus (either because every shard is empty, every line
  fails JSON parsing, or every parsed line lacks a conforming `ts`),
  BOTH `time_range.min` and `time_range.max` are JSON `null` — same
  encoding as default base behavior on an all-empty run.
- The corpus is **exactly** the same as the union of every
  `--sharding-report.shards[i].first_ts` / `shards[i].last_ts`
  contribution described in the sharding-report semantics. Consequently, when
  `--sharding-report` is ALSO emitted on the same run, the following
  closed-form invariants hold (see §4).

## 3b. `strict-observed` semantics (enforcement)

When `--summary-time-range-policy strict-observed` is supplied:

- Ingest runs the full existing pipeline (so `accepted` corpus and every
  existing sidecar are produced exactly as under the default policy).
- After every sidecar is finalized, the broader `observed` corpus is
  computed over the same input directory using the rules of §3.
- If the `observed` range and the `accepted` range COINCIDE (both
  min values string-equal and both max values string-equal, including
  the null/null case), the run succeeds with exit code 0. Every
  produced file is byte-identical to a run under
  `--summary-time-range-policy accepted`. `strict-observed` is purely
  a guard in this case.
- If the `observed` range and the `accepted` range DIVERGE (either the
  min differs or the max differs), the run FAILS with exit code `2`.
  All emitted output files are removed before the process exits — no
  `--output`, no `--rejects`, no `--state-out`, no `--audit-log`, no
  `--audit-log-digest`, no `--out-parquet-manifest`, no
  `--manifest-signature`, no `--summary-schema-out`, no
  `--summary-schema-digest`, no `--summary-schema-index`, no
  `--sharding-report`, no `--sharding-report-digest`, no
  `--sharding-report-index`, no `--sharding-report-histogram`, no
  `--run-provenance`, no `--run-provenance-digest`, and no `.tmp`
  companion of any of those. Stderr carries the exact line:

  ```
  ingest: --summary-time-range-policy: strict-observed divergence: accepted=[<a_min>,<a_max>] observed=[<o_min>,<o_max>]
  ```

  where each of `<a_min>`, `<a_max>`, `<o_min>`, `<o_max>` is either
  the literal RFC 3339 UTC string of the corresponding endpoint (no
  surrounding quotes) or the literal ASCII string `null` when that
  endpoint is JSON null on the corresponding side.

- `strict-observed` is equivalent to `accepted` (byte-identity) on any
  input where no invalid/late/duplicate/unresolved-chargeback line
  contributes a conforming `ts` outside the accepted window — i.e.
  exactly the inputs where `observed` would emit the same bytes as
  `accepted`.

- Under `strict-observed`, the provenance `flags` object records the
  argv token `"strict-observed"` under key `summary-time-range-policy`
  (verbatim) — same fixed enumeration position as the other values.

## 4. Invariants (strict)

1. **Default byte-identity**: when `--summary-time-range-policy` is
   absent, every existing output byte is identical to a run with
   the flag omitted. In particular `--output` is byte-identical to
   the default behavior on every input.
2. **`accepted` byte-identity**: when `--summary-time-range-policy
   accepted` is explicitly supplied, `--output` is byte-identical to
   the same run with the flag omitted. The observation that the flag
   was passed is NOT reflected in `--output`.
3. **`observed` scope**: when `--summary-time-range-policy observed`
   is supplied, only the two `time_range` value lines of `--output`
   can differ from the `accepted` variant. The key order
   (`schema_version`, `ingest_id`, `count_by_type`, `totals`,
   `accepted_by_day`, `rejected_by_reason`, `sessions`, `time_range`,
   `anomalies`), the indentation (2 spaces), and the trailing newline
   are unchanged.
4. **`observed` agrees with sharding-report when both are emitted**:
   when `--summary-time-range-policy observed` AND `--sharding-report
   <path>` are BOTH supplied on the same run with a non-empty input
   directory, let `reportMin` be the lexicographic min of
   `shards[i].first_ts` over all shards whose `first_ts` is non-null,
   and `reportMax` be the lexicographic max of `shards[i].last_ts`
   over all shards whose `last_ts` is non-null. Then:
   - If at least one shard has a non-null `first_ts`,
     `time_range.min == reportMin`.
   - If at least one shard has a non-null `last_ts`,
     `time_range.max == reportMax`.
   - If every shard has `first_ts == null` (and therefore `last_ts ==
     null`), both `time_range.min` and `time_range.max` are JSON
     `null`.
5. **Sidecar independence**: the presence or value of
   `--summary-time-range-policy` does NOT change `--rejects`,
   `--state-out`, `--audit-log`, `--audit-log-digest`,
   `--summary-schema-out`, `--summary-schema-digest`,
   `--summary-schema-index`, `--sharding-report`,
   `--sharding-report-digest`, `--sharding-report-index`, or
   `--sharding-report-histogram` bytes. These remain byte-identical to
   a run with `--summary-time-range-policy` omitted (everything else
   equal). If `--out-parquet-manifest` / `--manifest-signature` are
   emitted, their schemas are unchanged, but their bytes MAY differ
   under `observed` because they content-address the emitted
   `--output` summary bytes. If `--run-provenance` is emitted, its
   `flags` object records the explicit policy value, so
   `--run-provenance` bytes differ whenever the explicit policy value
   differs; `--run-provenance-digest` naturally follows the
   provenance bytes.
6. **Provenance recording**: when `--summary-time-range-policy
   <value>` is explicitly set AND `--run-provenance <path>` is
   supplied on the same run, the `flags` object inside the
   provenance sidecar includes the observed value under key
   `"summary-time-range-policy"` (string-valued; the argv token
   verbatim). The fixed enumeration order of §2.3 of the run-provenance feature is
   extended by appending `summary-time-range-policy` immediately
   after `run-provenance-strict` (see §7). When
   `--summary-time-range-policy` is NOT explicitly set, the key is
   OMITTED from the `flags` object (unchanged observed-only
   semantics via `fs.Visit`).
7. **`observed` broader than `accepted`** (implication, not a new
   axiom): on any input, the `observed` `time_range` span covers
   the `accepted` span. Concretely, if the `accepted` variant has
   `min == a_min` and `max == a_max` (both non-null), then the
